section_title: INTRODUCTION TO ICT CHARTER PRICE ACTION MODEL 7: UNIVERSAL TRADING
content:
MODEL
Welcome to Price Action Model Number Seven, the Universal Trading Model. This teaching specifically
deals with Sell-Side Low Resistance Liquidity Runs and Fractals. This model is applicable to every style of
trading:
- Position Trading
- Swing Trading
- Short-term Trading
- Day Trading
- Scalping
This model focuses on sell-side low resistance liquidity runs, utilizing the fractal. Our stage for this model is
a liquidity draw, meaning we are looking for a higher time frame level to absorb liquidity.

section_title: MODEL STAGE, SETUP, AND PATTERN DEFINITION
content:
The setup for this model is the Sell-Side Market Maker Profile. There are two Market Maker Profiles: a Buy
Model and a Sell Model. Both have a sell-side delivery, which is the portion of the model or profile that goes
lower seeking liquidity. This is what we will focus on for our setups. The pattern itself will refer to fair value,
which is a simple concept.
The sell-side expansion on a Market Maker Sell Model occurs when the chart, on any time frame, presents
a bearish condition. We must understand when the market is poised to move lower based on a draw on
liquidity. As depicted, the market will seek a level of liquidity, and once it reaches that level, it tends to move
lower if the higher time frame indicates it's feasible. This is how we begin stalking our setups.

section_title: TARGETED LIQUIDITY AND RANGE OF OPPORTUNITY
content:
Between the moment in price when we identify the liquidity the market will seek and the price level of the
targeted liquidity (i.e., after an initial run-up, we expect the market to go lower, so we target a liquidity level
below the market), this is our 'targeted liquidity'. The space between these two price points is our 'range of
opportunity'. For example, if we have equal lows, we know there's a sell-side liquidity pool resting below
that, which would be our targeted liquidity. The range of opportunity is clearly defined by these points, often
containing key liquidity areas.

section_title: MARKET MAKER SELL MODEL CURVE DYNAMICS
content:
Everything is connected to the original buy-side of the curve of our Market Maker Sell Model. Initially, we
observe a run-up, followed by a retracement back into the original consolidation. This is a return back to fair
value. We then see an expansion move up, and a trade back down. We do not look for classic support and
resistance theory here; instead, we wait for a retracement. Once the market provides another buying
opportunity here, it rallies up to an area of higher time frame liquidity. Once this liquidity is reached, and if
we are essentially bearish, we expect price to trade lower once we see the market break down below our
highest reaccumulation of Longs. Refer to Model Number Six for specific price points related to this
concept.

From the low formed, we draw a line through the curve, which represents the midpoint of the overall fractal.
If we project this out in time, the market will look to trade back to that level. Following this, there will be a
measure of distribution, meaning sell-side imbalances will start creating movement lower, often
characterized by large downward ranges. Many times, the market doesn't need to return to this specific
level. For lower time frames (e.g., 1-hour or 15-minute), they don't necessarily have to come back to this
point. If it doesn't, that's acceptable because the second opportunity typically creates a larger, more
protracted expansion on the downside, resulting in a larger price swing than just reaching below the
immediate liquidity.

section_title: IDENTIFYING SHORT ENTRY OPPORTUNITIES AND KEY LIQUIDITY LEVELS
content:
We are looking for opportunities to sell short in specific areas, initially targeting the liquidity below. If we
observe a significant run below this low, and it extends below the initial Point 1 of reaccumulation of Longs
on a Sell Model within this Market Maker Sell Profile, particularly if the market breaks down to this point.
Looking at the overall structure, every fractal and price swing will be slightly different, but the general theme
and rule of thumb is that the algorithm will seek this price point. If it runs through this point, meaning a price
leg goes all the way down and blows right through it, then once it retraces back up inside of the down
- closed candles in this swing low, we expect a large dynamic move farther than just this immediate liquidity
pool of sell stops.

section_title: WEEKLY LIQUIDITY DISTRIBUTION PROFILE AND SMART MONEY REVERSAL
content:
We are now looking at a Weekly Liquidity Distribution Profile, similar to what was shown in Price Action
Model Number Six. On the sell side, we have the last point of reaccumulation of Longs. We match this with
where the buy side was offered, meaning every time a bullish scenario was created to the left of the curve.
With the liquidity distribution profile, the algorithm seeks to pair up previous levels of buy-side and sell-side
delivery. During the buy-side of a Market Maker Sell Model, once it creates that 'Smart Money
Reversal' (SMR), it is important to note that this higher level of probability requires experience. It's not
about nailing the exact high, but rather focusing on opportunities that reside after the second
reaccumulation on the buy side of a Market Maker Sell Profile is broken on the right side of the curve.

I want you to focus on the opportunities that reside once the second reaccumulation of the buy side on a
Market Maker Sell Profile is broken on the right side of the curve. What we are going to be doing is looking
for the market to create a pairing of orders in the form of a Fair Value Gap, or where there were old sell
stops below that swing low that would have created a new reaccumulation. Those sell stops will get run out.
We want to see that take place. Once those sell stops are run out, then a return back up into this level of
liquidity in the form of the down-closed candles is where we look to pair our orders for going short because
there is going to be a form of mitigation there. In other words, they are going to knock out the Longs that
were rallying up and trying to make profit; they are stopped out. Then the market will return back to fair
value. The fair value is going to be determined by the down-close candles that make up the swing low to
the left of the curve of this overall expected price swing. The theory is we're expecting price to go up to
eventually go down. We watch price accumulate. Even if we weren't a buyer based on Price Action Model
Number Six, the power in this insight is that you don't have to be a buyer when the market trades up to this
level. Your whole profile and model may be looking for this structure to form, and once it breaks down and
trades below the second reaccumulation of the buy side of the curve on a Sell-Side Model, which will take
some time to fully grasp with Price Action Model Number Six and mentorship notes, we have a high
probability to see it seek the first initial reaccumulation of Longs.

section_title: REACCUMULATION POINTS AND ENTRY EXPERIENCE LEVELS
content:
From a theoretical standpoint, this would represent the higher of the two reaccumulation points. We have a
consolidation, it runs up, comes back down, finds the retracement back to the original consolidation (Point
One of reaccumulation of Longs). Then it rallies up, then retraces back down (the second buying
opportunity or reaccumulation of Longs). Then it rallies up to some level of buy-side liquidity to offset the
Longs that were accumulated. They are selling them here. You will not know without a great deal of
experience where this will be the reversal point. I am not teaching that because you have to look at things
like what is shown here, and over time and experience, that will teach you when to anticipate that smart
money reversal. There will be times you can drop a small position if you think a long-term high is forming.
Then, once it creates the scenario we are outlining here (the higher of the two swing lows prior to the
reversal), when that breaks, that's our Market Structure Break. We are not looking at a small, short-term
market structure break that creates the low-risk entry. The low-risk entry requires high experience. You get
that experience by doing what is being shown here. We are looking at the higher of the two reaccumulation
points. In some models, there may be three reaccumulations: one returns to consolidation, a second
reaccumulation doesn't reach the buy-side liquidity pool yet, and then a third one creates that. In that case,
we always use the higher between the original consolidation and where we thought the market would reach
for liquidity. Whichever the higher of the reaccumulation of Longs is seen, whatever that swing low is, we
want to see that be broken.

section_title: DEFINING MARKET STRUCTURE BREAK AND INSTITUTIONAL DELIVERY OF LIQUIDITY
content:
When the higher of the two swing lows prior to the reversal breaks, that's our Market Structure Break. We
are not going to look at small, short-term market structure breaks that create the low-risk entry; that
requires high experience. We are looking at the higher of the two reaccumulations. In some models, there
may be three reaccumulations. We always use the higher between the original consolidation and where we
thought that market would reach for liquidity. Whichever the higher of the reaccumulation of Longs is seen,
whatever that swing low is, we want to see that be broken. Once the market seeks the liquidity below that,
runs the sell stop below that on the left side of the curve, and once that occurs (I will show examples in
price), nonetheless, once the sell stops are taken, then we wait for fair value to be traded back to. In other
words, it could run the stops, then come back up into the bodies of the down-close candles of the swing
low that's formed here. Underline this in your notes: if the highest reaccumulation of Longs on the buy side
of the curve of a sell model (meaning price accumulated sideways, moves higher, trades up, and then goes
down), what we're looking for is that break in market structure of the highest of the reaccumulation of
Longs prior to a point of liquidity being taken out above an old high or some measure of a premium array.
Go back and look at the market maker models and overlap with the content from month number five where
we talk about the PD Array Matrix. We have that model of breaking market structure relative to a specific
price point; it's not just a general break. There is a very specific structure being shown here, and that's
what this liquidity distribution profile provides you. This slide is something that will not be shown by anyone
else; the content shown here is unique due to the institutional delivery of liquidity. That aspect of retail
learning and trading is void; it doesn't exist.

When individuals suggest they have an order flow background or understanding of supply and demand,
this is the missing link; they don't have this. Once we have an understanding of liquidity distribution, we
don't need charts, candlesticks, or any of that. We just need an old reference point in terms of price.
Traders who used this information off-floor (e.g., Floor Traders) would use the excitement and fever of
buying and selling around a specific level to get a feel for the order book as a whole by watching activity in
the pits. When everything transitioned to electronic trading, many struggled to adjust. I learned tape reading
as a commodity trader by watching CNBC (then FNN) and the ticker tape, seeing Futures Market updates
every 10 minutes. While it didn't give highs and lows, I would study it during the New York session for the
S&P and bond market, making notations to understand price delivery. I always felt price was controlled and
manipulated, despite being told markets were too big to manipulate. This understanding of liquidity
distribution provides the specific protocol for a market structure break. Many interpret 'market structure
break' loosely, but if it doesn't fit the criteria outlined here, then it's not a true break in market structure.
